sentences.text: Yes.
Well, all the family and her parents-- my little cousin-- her name was Lucinka.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] See, this will be the worst.
The-- her parents that was taken away-- and she only did hide somehow to-- when the-- no.
She was taken to the transport, too.
And she did have a terrific sense of survival.
She really did have the greatest will to live.
And before the transport was taken away from the place, she did smuggle herself under the wire to the non-Jewish section of the place.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my auntie-- and her auntie, did live then in the non-Jewish section-- not in the ghetto.
Because she married a Christian Polish doctor who did save her during the war.
And he really went through hell with her.
This Lucinka knew where they was.
She was about 6 then, maybe 6 and a 1/2.
So she remembered them, and she went there.
And my aunt was hiding as well.
But she did see her and didn't know where to put her.
She was too scared to take her to her place.
So she put her somewhere under the steps.
And then in a-- after a little while, the Polish people said, don't.
Take this child away from there.
If not, we're giving her away.
So after, I think, spending the night there under the steps, she-- she came back to ghetto.
She smuggled herself back to the ghetto.
And the ghetto was then in a liquidation.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I smuggled her to the concentration camp under my long coat.
She was a little girl then.
And we was marching in-- I don't know, six of-- I can't remember.
And she-- you know, I put her-- and she-- that's how I smuggled her in the Plaszow Concentration Camp.
There was still some children smuggled.
There were some children there, but not many.
She had a dreadful, dreadful life there.
She-- [SIGHS] there was always-- I did have to cover her with the blankets and make her invisible during the day so nobody knew.
And she did have to be under those blankets.
And I brought her whatever I can to feed her, and-- [CRYING] [SIGHS] [SOBBING] And she never complained.
She had such a strong will to live.
It's unbelievable.
And then, when I was taking all the children, because there was some children to-- they didn't take them-- what we later knew, it was to Auschwitz to-- they burned them.
And she was with-- with a few children.
They was hiding in the latrines, you know?
And she was hiding in the latrine, the poor darling.
It was a terrible condition, as you can imagine, you know.
And then they find all the children in the latrines, and they took them to the transports to Auschwitz.
But [SOBBING] she was so bright.
She really did want to live so much.
She was such a wonderful, intelligent child.
She really is a terribly sad memory for me.
[SIGHS] Oh, I can't stand it.
Her name was Lucinka Klein.
And I really-- she did die a martyr death.
And [SIGHS] for this, I hate the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Anyway, they took all the children to this transport.

And while they took the children, they was even playing a German song-- how to put the salt on the wound-- how a child asked the mother to give a little horsey to-- pony to the child, that the-- the child would think that would be a paradise.
That was the song they did sing while they took the children to the transport, you know?
It was that-- morally such a cruel thing.
It was indescribable.
Where were you at the time?
That was in Plaszow.
Yes, we was on appellplatz.
Everybody-- it was-- was the place where we all congregated.
They was calling everybody to the place so nobody could hide.
Everything that-- whatever was the rules, it was-- or you would be shot dead-- or you.
Give the jewelry or you would be shot dead.
Give whatever.
Do this and this, or you'll be shot dead.
In the camp, there was a lot of terrible thing.
This was a terrible thing.
Well, if you did see the film, it was only fiction-- just-- Goth.
It was the--
--was the boss of this-- an SS man who was the boss of this concentration camp.
He was absolute maniac.
Because it's not just to being inhuman.
I think he took a great pleasure of shooting people and-- whenever he could, you know?
There were some terrible things.
There was a lot of beating 20-- while somebody went to work and did-- want to bring some food or-- you know, it's very hard for me to do all this.
It's getting harder.
They was--
--beating.
They was-- the punishment was 25 lashes.
And then they did have to stand up.
And they was very powerful.
And-- and they did this-- the kidney.
Oh, I-- the kidneys was damaged throughout-- through such-- or lungs, or whatever, you know?
And then you'd have to stand up and say, thank you, and say their name, you know?
So some just-- After they were beaten?
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And--
The Goth.
Goth did give the order to do it-- to-- and a lot of Ukrainians usually did exercise this punishment, Ukrainian soldiers, which was what they called volksdeutsche-- if you heard of it-- that-- yeah.
Ah!
[SIGHS] God.
This is a torture.
You're filming all this?
It's terrible.
And--
Yes.
Well, in the camp, there was different work, compulsory different work.
Then I did work in a werkstatte, what was called, Schlosserei.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
That was a barrack in-- in a-- in a concentration camp, right?
There was some which was working at-- they was breaking some mountains and-- well, detonation, yes?
Is that what?
And there wasn't any-- there wasn't any security for their injuries or anything like this.
So very often, a leg would be torn.
Oh, I did see a lovely girl who-- whose leg was torn off.
And-- oh, God.
She was bleeding to death.
And before they did-- could give her any aid-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --and there was different werkstattes.
There was some-- what we'd sew uniforms.
There was some which did make brushes.
And what were you actually doing?
I was partially in a office of that Schlosserei.
And that Goth, he would come without any-- well, people did try not to work hard.
And he would come without any warning and ask, how many of this did you make?
Make one for me so I see, yeah?
[LAUGHS] And he would make one.
And he would try to make it as good as possible and as fast as possible.
And he said, ah.
It took 5 minutes or 10 minutes.
Because it was not enough.
Yes.
I also did see one young man who did come a bit late, and say, where you have been?
He didn't have a chance to give an answer.
I did see him shot through the head.
Not personally, no.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No, that was not.
That was in Plaszow.
Schindler did have Emalia, which was outside Plaszow, where-- actually, one of my auntie did work there.
And that was like, heaven by comparison with Plaszow.
And this is given in detail in the book and all that.
My husband was-- that's another story, you see.
My husband was in this Plaszow ghetto too-- in the Plaszow concentration camp as well.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
He was in charge of this Schlosserei, right?
And he did go through a dreadful hell, because he was responsible through-- for the people, you know?
And a lot of people did not want to try to do, you know?
And it was a shocking time.
What did he have to do?
Well, he did have to show that work is done.
And whatever discipline is-- should be upkept, you know?
And--
--it was very, very hard.
And I did see Himmler in person in my life--
I'm not sure about the third time.
Once he-- Himmler came to Plaszow ghetto.
And we did have to strip-- all people did have to strip naked-- completely naked.
Though we-- and it was a selection.
That was the most-- and he was just there standing, very powerful and joking with Goth, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
That was the most humiliating-- and I felt so sorry for the older people.
And, you know, they did try their best to look good, and-- [SIGHS]
And then the second time, I did see him in-- where I was selected, I could still stay, so-- [SIGHS] --and then slowly, of course-- well, Goth was-- I'm sure he was not a normal man.
Yet they describe him as intelligent man that time.
And-- but he certainly must have been some psychotic-- a maniac, you know, that he did not have feeling for-- any human feelings, you know?
He just-- I couldn't imagine that hate can be-- because what could the Jews do to him?
How powerful can hate be?
I'm-- I'm sure it was just some mania.
Anyway, as you know, he was hanged after the war.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Oh, dear.
And--
And then--
Well, it was-- it was bad.
And then it was-- that's chief.
He left.
They were shooting all the time.
There was hanging all the time.
There was lack of food.
There was-- it was a very, very-- and the biggest thing was the fear, the greatest fear of-- unexpected, out of the blue happens, you know?
Like that-- he was shot-- [NON-ENGLISH].
He was the-- in charge of the police, Jewish police, yes?
And-- it's terrible thing what was happening.
We did have a friend.
He was such a lovely man, a gorgeous-looking man.
His name was Samek Kempler.
And Goth's mistress-- he was in the cavalry-- ca-- cava-- cavler-- cavalery?
Cavalry.
Cavalry-- in Polish military.
And he-- she was looking for somebody who can teach her riding.
And somebody-- oh, I don't know how, he recommended him.
And he was on this white horse with her.
He did look so good, you know?
So that was a plot.
I don't know how the-- Goth did find out.
I don't know all the details.
But it was a plot that a Ukrainian told him that he would give him a gun.
And he make him-- it would make for him possible to escape.
And apparently, he went for this.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of course, it was all false.
And they shot him.
I would be-- I would say he must have been, because he was really-- and she was fond of him somehow, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Well, we did have this soup.
And some people was working in the kitchen to-- just peeling potatoes.
And it was a shortage of food.
Of course, it was.
I was hungry-- not all the time.
Definitely every scrap-- that's for sure.
I felt so sorry for that poor child.
The hygienic condition was so terrible, she couldn't-- didn't have possibility to wash properly.
And it was-- this poor child.
My health-- well, I got some infection of the bladder.
And it was a hospital.
It was really terrible, because some women-- when the ghetto was liquidated, they came to the camp pregnant, yes, and they-- coming from one to another.
But I have to mention that.
And I remember there was this doctor in the Jewish hospital.
I think they hang him after the war.
I can't recall his name now, because I'm too upset.
[SIGHS] And when the child was born, of course, you know, they did have to do so-- the poor thing.
He-- she was-- she couldn't scream.
And-- and-- and then it was a bucket of water.
And he did drown that newborn child in the bucket.
But he was terribly disturbed, saying, you're making me too nervous.
And he got hysterical and all this.
I don't know.
He did get such a bad name after that.
Well, I-- I didn't see that happening.
But I know it was happening.
And I was in hospital on-- once-- in one there-- one stage.
And it was this lady doctor, Dr.
Blau, I think was her name.
And they did get some medications through Red Cross.
But it-- that was all-- all in English.
And they was not-- I don't know.
She couldn't follow maybe the instruction, or-- and she gave me a great overdose of [NON-ENGLISH], what was-- the-- that was the medication-- what I remember was then.
And I was terribly poisoned.
I did think I'd die on that occasion.
Yes.
Oh, it was terrible.
It was a complete poisoning system with a collapse, you know.
You know, I must have been a very strong person that I survived that.
Do you know, right through the war-- I don't know what is in my makeup.
I always want to believe in a-- in a good-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --and best.
And right through this terrible time, the only thing which really kept my spirit is the hope.
I never somehow did see myself that this is what is destined for us, that it wouldn't come a better time, that this couldn't be reality-- that it must be only a temporary thing.
And I'm sure, on many occasion that in a very bad times-- and there was plenty of it-- this hope did carry me over.
And--
I beg your pardon?
No, I didn't.
I didn't.
I was very low at many times and very distressed.
But somehow, always, the little bit of spark of hope that this is not the real world, was in me, you know?
Yes, I did, in-- in Birkenau.
I did.
I inspired-- there was a transport from Greece coming.
There was some beautiful girls coming from Rhodes, from the island Rhodes.
And they came from the warm climate.
And then it was cold winter.
And there was no food.
And the family was taken to the gas chamber.
And-- [SIGHS] they-- they give up.
They gave up.
And I inspired one girl, which I particular took a liking to.
And I said-- because she didn't want to wash.
She didn't want to do anything, you know?
And I say, come on.
I take you to the latrine.
I wash you, you know?
You'll be OK.
You'll be OK.
And I-- I did try, but she was so resistant.
She only-- she wants is-- she was saying, Rhodes.
We didn't have a common language, because she was Greek.
She was saying, Rhodes, Auschwitz, Rhodes, Auschwitz.
And-- and what I understood, she did feel doomed.
And-- because her family-- they was very close family.
And they were so-- she knew they was-- gone.
And she didn't have the will to survive.
I-- I-- I think she went for a selection.
They all-- the whole group went to a selection.
They all went finally into the-- it was gas chambers, you know-- was cremated.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oh, God.
I should have inquired about that.
[LAUGHS] I think the ghetto was liquidated-- I think '42 was the liquidation of the ghetto.
And then-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] '44, I went to Birkenau.
So it must have been almost two years or close-- like that.
Well, the most terrible thing in Plaszow, was, I think, was-- hunger was one thing.
But it wasn't so much the hunger as the terrible fear of uncertainty and the terrible fear of being punished or shot for no reason, you know?
It was so unpredictable.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And-- no, no, that was the door.
Oh, my God.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Yes.
Well, I can, myself, recall such a-- I-- I-- I was hungry.
And it wasn't good nourishment.
But for myself, I managed with soup and whatever I can remember.
That wasn't-- the hunger is a-- was terrible fear and terrible thing what we did see.
Like, there was no crematoriums in Plaszow.
And they was bringing transport of people-- Jewish and non-Jewish-- and shooting them to the grave and burning them.
So the smell of-- you know, up in the air of these bodies was just absolutely unbelievable.
I-- I did-- I did see, on one occasion, where the mother did carry-- she had-- did have-- they did strip naked always.
And she did go walk with the child in her arm, with a infant, to the grave which was prepared.
And there was a woman's-- woman SS-- SS woman as well as men.
I-- one was pregnant.
And she was watching this for this mother going to the grave with a infant.
I mean, what sort of people was that, you know-- and you think?
And they was all shot in the neck and fell into the grave.
Once was a rabbi.
And he was shot, but I-- he wasn't fatally shot.
And he pretended to be dead.
And then he was like, dazed.
And after the execution, before they burned them, he was-- did come out.
But he was shot finally by the Ukrainian.
[LAUGHS] Absolutely a havoc-- moral havoc.
Whereas the camp-- were as a-- we knew that they not somehow doing well in-- on the Russian front.
There were some glimpses coming sometime of information from out--
I don't-- I think there was even some information that people did hear when the German was discussing.
Or it maybe came from Goth, because he did have the Jewish cook.
And you know-- with what's described in the book.
The information did sneak in from different area, but there was some information.
And also, as it came to a liquidation of the concentration camp, we heard from distant sound artillery, yes?
And then first, we did think it was our imagination.
But we did see this.
The camp was in liquidation.
And-- At this time--
--and then was the-- the list made-- as you know.
Oh, the people from Emalia did come to Plaszow as well.
And Schindler couldn't stop that.
They did have to come to the camp.
And not all the people which did work in the Emalia did come-- was put on a list.
Because my auntie, the one which is in Montefiore home, she was in Emalia.
And yet, she wasn't on the Schindler List.
And they was saying, there was a lot of corruption who was on the list and whatever.
But I must say, in all honesty, what I know, it wasn't any corruption on my part.
I was in-- on the list.
Well, there was a-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] There's the door-- the door.
And there was rumors, yes, there was rumors that it's a list made.
There is a list made.
And everybody did want to be on the list.
It was a big havoc.
It was very chaotic and undisciplined.
I-- I really don't very-- I haven't been close to those people making the list.
Or I don't know much about it.
But I was on the list.
Must-- somebody must have mentioned my name.
I-- because we was taking as one lot-- oh, actually not really.
The list was really to-- to me, the list I heard about was in Birkenau.
But people were saying there-- there was a list made, and Schindler is supposed to-- but it took quite a long time.
And this was the worst time, because I was really not well.
I was sick.
I had a cold.
I was distressed.
That when I was really very, very much down.
So don't ask me even how long I was in Birkenau, 'cause I have to ask somebody else and rely on somebody else.
[SIGHS]
I told her to be here about 12:00.
Well--
--my son can still-- can I see him for a minute?
